refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' (a boundary condition) and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated metric rather than a fixed value.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct end-to-end packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, congestion profiles) agreed with customer. Measure across multiple teleoperation sessions and load scenarios. Acceptance: measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined target (default 1%) with 95% confidence over a statistically significant sample.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation subsystem shall implement packet loss detection and reporting.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation subsystem shall employ error correction or retransmission mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on command/feedback integrity.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation subsystem shall adapt communication parameters (e.g., compression, redundancy) based on network conditions to maintain packet loss within acceptable bounds.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The safety services subsystem shall monitor teleoperation communication quality and trigger safe-state transitions if packet loss exceeds threshold.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement adaptive forward error correction (FEC) and selective retransmission in the teleoperation protocol stack, with real-time packet loss monitoring and dynamic codec/compression adjustment based on measured network conditions.
DP-B: Deploy redundant communication channels (dual network paths or multi-carrier) with packet aggregation and voting logic to mask individual channel packet loss.
DP-C: Implement application-level acknowledgment and timeout-driven retransmission with exponential backoff, combined with operator feedback on communication quality (visual/audio indicators).

DP-D: Hybrid approach combining FEC for latency-sensitive telemetry (DP-A) with selective retransmission for non-time-critical tasking commands (DP-C), and safety-critical alerts routed through redundant channels (DP-B).
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Hybrid adaptive communication strategy—FEC for telemetry streams (low latency tolerance), selective retransmission for tasking commands (moderate tolerance), and redundant channels for safety alerts (zero tolerance for loss). Network condition monitoring drives dynamic parameter tuning (codec selection, FEC strength, retransmission timeout) to maintain packet loss ≤ threshold.

potential_sources: Coupling between telemetry FEC parameters and operator latency perception—must validate that FEC overhead does not introduce unacceptable command-response delay.
Coupling between redundant channel logic and network cost/bandwidth constraints—dual-path routing may exceed available bandwidth in some customer deployments.
Coupling between safety alert redundancy and failover logic—must ensure that redundant paths do not create race conditions or conflicting safety commands.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is modular: each sub-strategy (FEC, retransmission, redundancy) can be independently tuned and validated. Coupling is primarily at the network condition monitoring layer, which must coordinate parameter selection across all three strategies. Recommend explicit interface contracts between monitoring logic and each strategy module.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer-agreed network conditions are stable and representative of actual deployment scenarios. Risk: If real-world conditions differ significantly, packet loss may exceed threshold despite meeting acceptance criteria in lab/agreed conditions.

Assumption: FEC and retransmission overhead (latency, bandwidth) are acceptable within operator workload and network capacity constraints. Risk: Excessive overhead may degrade user experience or exceed available bandwidth.

Assumption: Redundant channels (if deployed) are sufficiently independent to avoid correlated failures. Risk: If both channels share common infrastructure (e.g., same ISP backbone), redundancy provides limited benefit.
Assumption: Safety alert redundancy does not introduce command conflicts or race conditions. Risk: Poorly coordinated failover could result in duplicate or contradictory safety commands.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) packet loss thresholds for each stream type (telemetry, tasking, safety alerts) and corresponding strategy selection logic; (2) network condition monitoring metrics (loss rate, latency, jitter) and update frequency; (3) failover and recovery procedures for redundant channels; (4) operator notification strategy when packet loss approaches threshold. Add acceptance criteria for latency impact of FEC/retransmission overhead.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end communication protocol stack, including packet loss detection, adaptive FEC/retransmission logic, network condition monitoring, codec/compression selection, and redundant channel management. Interfaces with Safety Services for safety alert routing and with Vehicle for telemetry/tasking payload formatting.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module outputs telemetry streams (sensor data, vehicle state) to remote operator console. Packet loss on this interface impacts operator situational awareness. FEC applied to reduce loss impact.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives tasking commands (movement, equipment control) from remote operator. Selective retransmission applied to ensure command delivery. Latency-sensitive; retransmission timeout must be tuned to operator expectations.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module routes safety alerts (emergency stop, hazard warnings) with redundant channel support. Bidirectional: receives alerts from Safety Services, transmits to operator console. Zero-loss tolerance; redundancy mandatory.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module interfaces with underlying network infrastructure (WiFi, cellular, satellite, or hybrid). Monitors link quality metrics (loss, latency, jitter) and adapts communication parameters accordingly.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of packet loss mitigation logic. All FEC, retransmission, redundancy, and network monitoring functions are core to teleoperation protocol design. Minimal diffusion: only safety alert routing requires coordination with Safety Services (via SafetyAlert interface).

Safety Services could own safety alert redundancy logic, but packet loss mitigation for general telemetry and tasking is outside its scope. High diffusion risk if Safety Services owns communication quality—would require deep coupling with network layer and codec selection, violating separation of concerns.

Vehicle module owns sensor/actuator interfaces and local control logic, not communication protocol. Assigning packet loss mitigation to Vehicle would create tight coupling between vehicle firmware and network protocol, reducing modularity and testability.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container but not the appropriate owner for detailed packet loss mitigation. Assigning to NGHE Solution would bypass the Teleoperation module and create architectural ambiguity about where communication logic resides.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the clear owner of packet loss mitigation. It already owns the communication protocol stack and network interface. Packet loss detection, FEC, retransmission, and network monitoring are core teleoperation functions. Safety alert redundancy is coordinated via the SafetyAlert interface, maintaining clean separation of concerns.
containment_risks: Risk: If network conditions are highly variable or customer-specific, Teleoperation module may require frequent tuning/reconfiguration. Mitigation: Design network condition monitoring as a pluggable subsystem with customer-configurable thresholds.

Risk: Redundant channel logic may require hardware-level support (dual network interfaces, carrier aggregation) that is not available in all deployment scenarios. Mitigation: Make redundancy optional; design fallback to single-channel FEC/retransmission for resource-constrained deployments.

Risk: Safety alert redundancy coordination between Teleoperation and Safety Services could create hidden coupling if not carefully managed. Mitigation: Define explicit SafetyAlert interface contract with clear ownership of failover logic (recommend Safety Services owns failover decision, Teleoperation owns channel management).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify ownership of safety alert failover logic: recommend that Safety Services owns the decision to trigger failover (based on packet loss threshold breach), while Teleoperation owns the mechanism (redundant channel switching). Document this split ownership in the SafetyAlert interface specification. Additionally, define a 'Network Condition Monitor' sub-module within Teleoperation with explicit APIs for querying link quality and updating communication parameters.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid adaptive communication) directly addresses FR-NGHE-009 by combining FEC, retransmission, and redundancy strategies tailored to different packet loss sensitivities. The DP is traceable to the FR and includes explicit mechanisms (network monitoring, parameter tuning) to maintain packet loss ≤ threshold. No independence violations detected; FEC, retransmission, and redundancy are orthogonal strategies that can be independently controlled.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk (Low): Safety alert redundancy requires coordination with Safety Services module. Mitigation: SafetyAlert interface clearly delineates responsibilities (Teleoperation manages channels, Safety Services manages failover decisions).

Diffusion Risk (Low): Network condition monitoring may require access to vehicle telemetry and operator feedback. Mitigation: Design monitoring as a passive observer of Telemetry and Tasking interfaces; avoid bidirectional coupling.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 95% containment score for DP-D. All core packet loss mitigation logic (FEC, retransmission, redundancy, network monitoring) is naturally contained within the teleoperation protocol stack. External dependencies are limited to SafetyAlert interface (for safety alert routing) and Network Link interface (for underlying network access). Containment is clean and defensible.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-009 is feasible via end-to-end packet loss measurement under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module can expose packet loss metrics (real-time and historical) via telemetry interface. Acceptance testing can be conducted in lab (simulated network conditions) and field (real deployment). Verification is straightforward and measurable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of FR-NGHE-009 to Teleoperation module is clear. Sub-FRs (packet loss detection, error correction, parameter adaptation, safety monitoring) are all allocated to either Teleoperation or Safety Services with explicit interface contracts. No ambiguity about ownership or responsibility.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to specify packet loss thresholds for each stream type (telemetry, tasking, safety alerts) and corresponding strategy selection logic. Current DP is generic; customer-specific tuning parameters should be documented.
Add explicit acceptance criteria for latency impact of FEC/retransmission overhead. DP-D may introduce additional latency; verify that operator experience remains acceptable.
Document failover and recovery procedures for redundant channels. Current DP assumes redundancy is available; clarify behavior when redundant channels are not deployed.
Define Network Condition Monitor sub-module within Teleoperation with explicit APIs for querying link quality and updating communication parameters. This will improve modularity and testability.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic and requires further decomposition. The refined FR identifies four sub-FRs (packet loss detection, error correction, parameter adaptation, safety monitoring) that must be individually designed and verified. DP-D is sound and well-justified, but its implementation requires detailed design of each sub-strategy (FEC algorithm selection, retransmission timeout tuning, redundancy failover logic, network monitoring metrics). Teleoperation module is the correct owner, but the module boundary must be refined to clarify sub-module responsibilities (e.g., Network Condition Monitor, FEC Engine, Retransmission Manager, Redundancy Controller). Recommend decomposing to the next level to design each sub-strategy and sub-module.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation subsystem shall detect and report packet loss in real-time, with measurement granularity sufficient to distinguish loss on telemetry, tasking, and safety alert streams.

FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation subsystem shall apply forward error correction (FEC) to telemetry streams with configurable FEC strength (e.g., Reed-Solomon code rate) to maintain packet loss impact ≤ 0.1% on operator-visible metrics.

FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation subsystem shall implement selective retransmission for tasking commands with adaptive timeout tuning based on measured round-trip latency, ensuring command delivery probability ≥ 99.9%.

FR-NGHE-009.4: The teleoperation subsystem shall support redundant communication channels (dual network paths or multi-carrier) for safety alerts, with automatic failover when primary channel packet loss exceeds threshold.

FR-NGHE-009.5: The teleoperation subsystem shall monitor network conditions (loss, latency, jitter) and dynamically adjust communication parameters (codec selection, FEC strength, retransmission timeout) to maintain overall packet loss ≤ customer-defined threshold.

assumptions: Customer-agreed network conditions are stable and representative of actual deployment scenarios.
FEC and retransmission overhead (latency, bandwidth) are acceptable within operator workload and network capacity constraints.
Redundant channels (if deployed) are sufficiently independent to avoid correlated failures.
Safety alert redundancy does not introduce command conflicts or race conditions.
Teleoperation module has access to network quality metrics (loss, latency, jitter) from underlying network infrastructure.
Operator console can display communication quality indicators (packet loss, latency) to inform operator of network conditions.
open_risks: Risk: Real-world network conditions may differ significantly from agreed conditions, causing packet loss to exceed threshold in deployment. Mitigation: Conduct field testing in representative environments; design adaptive parameter tuning to handle unexpected conditions.

Risk: FEC/retransmission overhead may introduce unacceptable latency, degrading operator experience. Mitigation: Set explicit latency budgets for each stream type; validate in user acceptance testing.
Risk: Redundant channels may not be available in all customer deployments (cost, infrastructure constraints). Mitigation: Design redundancy as optional; provide fallback single-channel strategy.
Risk: Safety alert failover logic may create race conditions or conflicting commands if not carefully coordinated. Mitigation: Define explicit failover procedures and test extensively.
Risk: Network condition monitoring may introduce overhead that consumes bandwidth/CPU. Mitigation: Design monitoring as lightweight; use sampling or aggregation to reduce overhead.
evidence_needed: Measurement data: Packet loss rates under representative network conditions (WiFi, cellular, satellite, hybrid) with varying load, latency, and jitter profiles.
Validation data: End-to-end latency measurements with FEC/retransmission enabled; confirm operator experience remains acceptable.
Test results: Redundant channel failover testing; confirm no race conditions or conflicting commands.
Customer input: Confirmation of agreed network conditions and packet loss threshold (default 1% or customer-specific target).
Design documentation: Detailed specification of FEC algorithm, retransmission timeout tuning, redundancy failover logic, and network monitoring metrics.
Field trial data: Packet loss measurements in actual deployment scenarios; confirm DP-D achieves acceptance criteria.
